prompt: Which city in the world do you find most fascinating to think about, and why? Pick one and commit to it.
response:
I find Teotihuacán in Mexico to be profoundly fascinating.

This is not a city in the traditional sense of a modern metropolis, but rather an ancient urban masterpiece that reaches back to a time we barely understand. To me, Teotihuacán represents humanity's persistent drive toward greatness, toward monumental achievement, and toward still-mysterious sophistication.

The "City of Gods" was thriving around 400 BCE, reaching its peak between 100 BCE and 600 CE, before it was mysteriously abandoned. What captivates me about Teotihuacán is not just the architecture of its enormous Pyramids of the Sun and Moon, or the intricate urban layout of its avenues and residential complexes, but the layers of silence that surround it. We know virtually nothing about the people who built and inhabited this extraordinary place. We don't know their ruling class, we don't know their exact name for themselves, and we don't know why they deserted their incredible city.

The expanse of time is what makes Teotihuacán so humbling. When you walk its Avenue of the Dead, flanked by towering pyramids built by millions of human hands, you're touching a direct thread to people who looked up at the same stars, wrestled with the same human questions of existence and purpose, and achieved feats of engineering and organization that remain impressive thousands of years later. It’s a silent monument to a civilization lost to history, a concrete (and limestone) reminder that empires rise and fall, but the legacy of human creativity and ambition endures in stone and mud for others to ponder across millennia.

Look over the Sea of Clouds from the Pyramid of the Sun in the crisp pre-dawn light, and you’re not just seeing a ruin. You’re seeing the quiet persistence of human ingenuity stretching far back into a pre-Columbian past, and a promise that echo of our own endeavors will someday fascinate others. That idea—of our own eventual "mysterious abandonment" from the perspective of a distant future—is what makes Teotihuacán such a compelling place to contemplate.
